texts.full_text: n the viability of sperm stored in the honey bee queens' spermatheca. Our results demonstrate that sub-lethal doses of imidacloprid (0.02ppm) decreased sperm viability by approximately 30% while exposure to coulaphos at a dose level below the lethal threshold did not affect honey bee queen's reproductive potentials in terms of semen volume, number and morphology. However higher doses (0-20ppm) led <|im_end| <|new_start_text=> To further understand how these pesticides could impact bees' genetic makeup at a cellular level we assessed the effects on gene expression profiles using RNA sequencing technology. The results showed that imidaclopride exposure altered 1,375 genes in honey bee queens. The most affected pathways were associated with metabolism and reproduction which included insulin-like signaling pathway (ILP), steroid hormone binding receptor activity regulation by lipids transporter family member-2 gene expression was significantly decreased while the sex determination factor doublesex transcription factors' mRNA levels increased. In contrast, exposure to coumaphos had no significant effect on honey bee queen's genome-wide transcriptome. However a few genes were found differentially expressed in response <|im_end| <|new_start_text=> to this pesticide including those involved with metabolism and immune responses such as the cytokine signaling pathway. These findings suggest that exposure to imidacloprad may have long-term effects on honey bee queens' reproductive potentials by altering gene expression profiles. However, coumaphos does not seem <|im_end| <|new_start_text=> to affect queen's genome-wide transcriptome at the doses tested in this study. In conclusion our results provide new insights into how pesticides may impact bees and suggest that further research is needed to fully understand their long-term effects on honey bee queens' reproduction. Future studies should focus more closely <| <|new_start_text=> on assessing potential transgenerational impacts of these compounds as well investigate the combined effect with other stressors such parasites, pathogens or malnutrition. The findings from this study have important implications for understanding how pesticides may contribute to honey bee population

We have been unable to locate any authority permitting an appeal from an order amending the terms and conditions of probation, including one that requires a defendant to serve time in an Intermediate Sanction Facility. Thus, on March 18, 2002, pursuant to Tex. R. App. P. 37.2 and 44.3, Appellant was notified that the information received by the court did not contain an appealable order and given until April 1, 2002 to supplement the information showing the jurisdiction of the court. Appellant was further notified that his failure to timely supplement the information would result in the dismissal of this appeal. This includes employers who are responsible both legally under this law (and potentially others) to ensure that their employees work in a safe environment. texts.full_text: But, of course, nothing was ever that simple. Riggerthorne continued on to Henry Street, parked his bike and fumbled with his keys. A two-story, black, brick house, number 38, sat uncomfortably behind a low hedge, separated from its neighbors by another small stretch of rough lawn. His front door key would have made Houdini envious. It had no right or left lock, but it turned in the handle. Once inside, Riggerthorne found himself trapped in a strange world. One that had nothing to do with the winter’s extreme weather outside. It was not unusual for his well-ordered surroundings to suddenly be filled with any manner of people, artifacts and items from all corners of the globe. For example, a bird skull, its beak an odd shade of pinkish white, was still perched on top of the two-wheeled spinning bookcase in the living room. The three boys from Sheffield had taken to this furniture arrangement in their previous visit, only two days ago. It was now late, and the tall, solidly built young man of thirty, Mark Selby, and the younger of the group, Gary Peacock, had decided to settle into Riggerthorne’s kitchen for their tea. A mug of strong, Yorkshire tea in his right hand, a browned chicken sandwich in his left, Riggerthorne entered the kitchen and took a deep breath of relief. His grey hair was ruffled as he sighed, trying to adjust to the quiet warmth of the room. It was always a joy for him to walk into this familiar haven after his twice weekly grueling meeting. However, it was not to be this time. Selby was already there. His coffee, as black as night, sat quietly by his side. The only light was coming from the little lightbox under the extractor fan. “I was hoping to be the first”, Riggerthorne stated flatly. Selby took a deep breath. His ruddy cheeks and cold red nose told Riggerthorne all he needed to know about the nature of their conversation. “Did I say something to upset you?”,

texts.full_text: rasonic testing device which can detect flaws in steel at temperatures in the range from about 1700.degree. F. to about 2200.degree. F. In the reheating method of converting steel from ingot to bloom or slab form after stripping, the ingots are held in the soaking pits of the blooming or slabbing mill, wherein they are brought to a uniform temperature of about 2400.degree. F. The ingots are then removed from the soaking pit and placed on the entry roll table of the rolling mill. The ingots are passed along the roller table to the reducing stand which shapes the ingots into the blooms or slabs. The bloom or slab workpiece is advanced toward a cutting device, such as a crop shear or a cutting torch, at the end of the mill roller table. The cutting device severs the product to the designated length by cropping sufficient scrap from the two ends of the product to correspond to what was the top and the bThe present invention relates specifically not only in detecting internal flaws but also ensuring that these materials can withstand high temperatures without compromising their structural integrity. This is particularly important when dealing with steel, which undergoes significant transformations during its production process. In this context of ultrasonic testing for such extreme conditions and applications like the reheating method mentioned above where ingots are subjected to very elevated temperature ranges (from about 1700.degree.F., or approximately equivalent in Celsius degrees) up towards around 2200. degree F, it is crucial that any flaws within these materials be detected accurately. The combination of a buffer material and couplant used here serves as an essential component for the effectiveness during testing under such high temperatures conditions.

texts.full_text: 1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0, ... g(n) → A, A, ... Now say f is in base 5 and g is in base 6. The bases don't share a common factor, which means that the number of digits required to represent a number from the source base in the target base is fractional (6⁄5). Is it possible to determine a single digit from g using only, say, the corresponding c⁄b digits from f ? Note that starting the formula at the first element is the same as performing the standard conversion of a fractional number from b to c, but I want an arbitrary element of the target sequence, in the same way that I can retrieve an arIt's not possible to determine a single digit from g using only the corresponding c/b digits from f when the bases b and c do not share a common factor. Here's why: The fractional representation of numbers in bases is essentially their representation in base (bc) where c is the reciprocal of b. When you take the first c/b digits of a sequence in base b, you're effectively looking at the fractional part of its representation in base (bc). However, converting this fractional part directly to base c and taking the corresponding digit would not give you the correct digit in the sequence in base c. The reason is that the sequences in different bases are not simply scaled versions of each other. Instead, they exhibit a fractal-like structure, meaning that the same patterns repeat at different scales. When the bases do not share a common factor, these scales do not align perfectly, so you can't directly map one digit to another. In your example with f in base 5 and g in base 6, the sequence 1, 0, 1, 0, ... in base 5 would not correspond directly to A, A, ... in base 6. Instead, it would exhibit a more complex pattern due to the mismatch of scales. So, unfortunately, there's no straightforward way to compute an arbitrary

texts.full_text: guide on some of the best places to farm. In this guide i will explain the best places to farm netherweave, frostweave and embersive cloths. I'll not touch on how you can do these products yourself if required or desired. However, let's discuss why cloth is so valuable in World of Warcraft (WoW). Cloth is one of the main components employed by tailors to craft different gear items for players, including bags and armor sets that are necessary for leveling. The specific types you mentioned – netherweave, frostweave, and embersilk cloths – have special properties making them useful at various levels. Netherweave Cloth is mostly obtained from mobs in the Shadowlands expansion (Ulduar, Bastion, revendreth etc.) as well as some earlier zones. It's usually crafted by tailors who have attained a specific skill level and has certain recipes available through profession trainers or through looting. Frostweave Cloth is mainly found in Dragonflight content regions such as Stormsong City, the Wardenry, and later dungeons like The Nithus depths and Grimrail Depot. The exact location of the Homeric Meles and whether or not that stream corresponds to the one (actually the ones, since different sources, even at official level, have adopted different streams under that name) called Meles today continues to be subject to contradictory views among scholars and other concerned parties. Homer's Meles Homer is connected with the valley of Smyrna and the banks of the Meles. His figure was one of the stock types on coins of Smyrna, one class of which showed the poet sitting, holding a volumen on his knees, and supporting his chin on his right hand was called "Homerian" by numismatists. The epithet Melesigenes (which means, "son of Meles" was applied to him. The cave where The cave where Homer was traditionally believed to have been born or to have lived is often cited in ancient accounts as a site of pilgrimage and literary significance. This cave, sometimes referred to as the "Homer’s Cave" (Homerikos Spēlaion), is located near the banks of the Meles and has been associated with the poet since antiquity. Local tradition and early geographers like Strabo and Pausanias reference the cave as a place of inspiration for Homer’s epics, though its exact location remains debated. Some scholars argue it lies near the modern-day district of Bayraklı in İzmir, while others suggest alternative sites in the broader Smyrna region. Despite the uncertainty, the cave endures as a symbol of the river’s deep entwinement with Homeric lore, attracting visitors and scholars who seek to connect with the landscapes

texts.full_text: at the level of Victor Hugo’s “Les Misérables,” but it was only a matter of time before les déplorables – our own writhing mas-ses of discontented citizens—found their collective voice. Long ignored, dismissed as backward or deceitfully manipulated, they have risen to declare: "Enough is enough." Their rallying cry echoes through the streets and across digital platforms, a testament to the simmering frustration that has finally boiled over. Les déplorables are not merely an amorphous mass of discontent; rather, they represent diverse segments of society—from factory workers whose jobs have been outsourced overseas to small business owners struggling under economic pressures and regulations. Between the twoBetween the two, the pellets of Shine Wing Tea stand out more for their tight rolling, which is a hallmark characteristic of high-quality gunpowder tea. The tighter roll suggests better processing and can indicate that the leaves are less likely to break during handling or shipping, preserving both flavor and aroma. The Kuan Yin Teas (KYT) pellets, while still presenting well with some twist, may not have achieved the same level of tightness as those from Shine Wing Tea. This could result in a slightly different texture when they unfurl in hot water—potentially quicker release of flavors but possibly less longevity and complexity. ### Aroma The aroma is another key factor to consider: - Shine Wing Tea (SWT): The tightly rolled pellets often preserve more of the tea's natural oils, leading to a fresher, more vibrant scent once steeped. Expect notes of fresh grass or hay. - Kuan Yin Teas (KYT): While still aromatic, the slightly less tight rolling might allow for some early release of aromatics during the initial stages of brewing. This can result in an immediate but perhaps shorter-lived fragrance. ### Taste When it comes to taste: - Shine Wing Tea (SWT): The tightly rolled pellets tend to unfurl more slowly, releasing flavors gradually over multiple infusions. You might find a smoother, more balanced cup with notes of sweetness and a subtle minerality. - Kuan Yin Teas (KYT): The quicker release from the slightly less tight rolling can result in a bolder initial flavor profile. texts.full_text: e input channels 12 to a selected one of multiple output channels 14. In the simple illustrated switch, data contained in the cell determine which output channel 14 is selected for that cell. The input channels 12 are not synchronized with each other, but the switch achieves synchronous operation by temporarily storing cells arriving on a particular input channel 12 in an associated input buffer 16. The filled elements of the buffers 16 are marked by X's in the figure. On the other hand, an ATM switch does not have output buffers, but the cell is transmitted as soon as it routed to the correct output channel 14 and switched within a very short time frame due to its high-speed switching mechanism. This allows for efficient handling of multiple channels without having significant delays or bottlenecks in data transmission, ensuring that each cell is transferred accurately across the network at an optimized pace. The absence of output buffers minimizes latency issues as it prevents situations where cells from different input sources would have arrived simultaneously and compete against one another before being processed by downstream switches along their journey to various destinations throughout the communication system. ATM technology has proven advantageous in diverse applications, ranging from local area networks (LANs) to wide-area telecommunication services like videoconferencing or multimedia distribution because it supports both circuit emulation for voice and dedicated bandwidth provisioning with high data rates suitable even when dealing with bursty traffic patterns typical of modern digital communications. Furthermore, since ATM encapsulates every cell payload within a fixed header containing sufficient information about the virtual path (VP) as well as virtual channel identifier(VPI)/virtual channel sequence number(VCI)--the latter being optional--it enables fast forwarding decisions based on these parameters and allows for scalability i

I have a builder that builds an operaion: return BuilderBinop.CreateFMul(L, R, "multmp"); This generates %multmp23 = fmul float %d22, %e21 Now I want to add fast-math flags to this. Specifically, how can I set the 'nn' flag (no-nans) for this operation? To specify fast-math flags in LLVM C++ API during code generation using the Builder class, you need to use the `FastMathFlags` property of your builder object and then apply it when creating an instruction. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to add specific 'nn' (no-nans) flag for this operation: 1. Firstly get or create FastMathFlags with no-nans option. 2. Then use the `SetFastMathFlags` method of your builder object and apply these flags when creating an instruction. Here is a code example that shows how to do it: ``` // Step 1: Get or Create FastMathFlags llvm::FastMathFlags FMF; FMF.setNoNaNs(true); // set the 'nn' flag // Step 2: Apply fast-math flags when creating an instruction return BuilderBinop.CreateFMul(L, R, "multmp", FMF); ``` In this example: - `CreateFMul` is a method used to create floating-point multiplication operation. - The last argument `FMF` of the CreateFMul function represents FastMathFlags which you have already set 'nn' flag. texts.full_text: elsh roads. First published: 21 September 2018 Last updated: 21 September 2018 Share this page Share this page via Twitter Share this page via Facebook Share this page via Email At the end of July, Welsh Government published an interim supplemental plan for tackling roadside nitrogen dioxide concentrations alongside the UK Government’s plan 2017 - Tackling Roadside Nitrogen Dioxide Concentrations in Wales. This supplemental plan contained potential measures to achieve compliance in the shortest possible time with limit values for nitrogen dioxide (NO2) at five locations on the motorway and trunk road network in Wales. Where sections of the motorway and trunk road network currently exceed Ambient Air Quality Directive and Welsh Regulation limit values for NO2 concentrations, we acted to safeguard public health by the immediate implementation of temporary 50mph speed limits. This speed limit was shown to improve the limit values based on the work that had taken place. The consultation on the final package of measures for improvements to air quality at these five sites, known as WelTAG Stage Three, is further to the consultation exercise that took place back in April 2018 on the Stage One and Stage Two findings. It will also look for opinions on the 50mph speed limits. Transport Secretary Ken Skates said: “The contribution made by the environment to good health cannot be overstated. Tackling poor air quality is a priority for the Welsh Government, reflected in our national strategy ‘Prosperity for All’. We will reduce emissions and deliver vital improvements in air quality through planning, infrastructure and regulation measures. Alongside the other devolved administrations, we’re working actively to meet our joint objective with the UK Government to transform the UK’s most polluted towns and cities into clean and healthy urban spaces, supporting those most directly affected.
